upperAir: No KOAK sounding data was available for today. Proxy inference from surface METARs and model BL heights: the boundary layer is shallow all morning (40–260m at Mussel Rock, 115–365m at Ed Levin), suggesting a persistent low-level inversion suppressing thermal development. BL height grows slowly through the afternoon to ~450m at Ed Levin and ~320m at Mussel Rock, indicating a modest thermal ceiling well below 2000ft MSL. The KHAF METARs overnight showed strong NNW flow gusting to 29–30 kts at Half Moon Bay airport, confirming an active pressure gradient driving offshore/gradient wind rather than a clean sea breeze pattern.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the SF Bay Area region. However, KHAF METARs are alarming: sustained NW winds 19–22 kts with gusts to 27–30 kts through the overnight and into early morning (0055Z), with haze reducing visibility to 5–6SM. KSFO logged a peak wind of 30 kts at 2316Z. These are pre-dawn/overnight readings but indicate a strong gradient wind event that likely persists into the morning hours. Pilots should treat this as a wind warning for all coastal and ridge-adjacent sites.

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site that works on W to NW sea breeze or gradient wind lifting over the coastal bluff. Ideal surface winds are 10–20 kts from 270–315°. The site is sensitive to rotor from terrain below and to cross/onshore flow that goes too strong or too north.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor today is the transition from an overnight gradient wind event (KHAF gusting 27–30 kts NNW) to a lighter midday sea breeze — but the early window is dangerously strong and the late window is marginal at best. KHAF was reporting 20–22 kts sustained with 29–30 kt gusts just hours before dawn; that gradient wind will still be affecting the coast at launch time. By the Open-Meteo forecast, surface winds at Mussel Rock ease to 4–7 kts from the W/WNW through midday, which sounds light but the overnight METARs suggest model timing may be optimistic. BL height stays very shallow (40–255m) all day, suppressing any thermal contribution. Cloud cover is erratic — 100% at 10 AM and 12 PM, clearing to 28–33% by 2–4 PM. The afternoon sees winds building back to 9–10 kts from 262–279° with gusts implied by the overall gradient pattern. Overall this is a tricky, potentially punchy day with unreliable wind strength and heavy overcast killing any thermal assist.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds light at 4–6 kts W but the overnight KHAF gradient event (20+ kts gusting 30) is too recent to trust the model's calm numbers. 87–100% cloud cover at 10–11 AM. Not a safe morning launch window.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 6.4–7.8 kts from 261–268° — on paper this is soarable W flow, but cloud cover swings from 100% to 1% to 100%, suggesting unstable air masses moving through. Marginal ridge lift possible but inconsistent.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds increase to 9.6–10.1 kts from 262–282° with cloud returning to 90–99% by 5 PM. Strongest and most consistent ridge wind of the day, but building strength and returning overcast complicate the picture. Risk of conditions exceeding comfortable limits.

verdict.hazards: Overnight gradient wind event (30 kt gusts at KHAF) may linger into morning; erratic cloud cover 0–100%; shallow BL with potential rotor; wind building late afternoon

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — low launch on Mission Peak ridge, SW-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 300 ft launch works on a thermal/valley breeze combination with SW to W winds. It is the most sheltered of the Ed Levin launches and works best when the valley sea breeze fills in from the SW, providing both lift and a safe landing direction. Surface winds ideally 5–12 kts SW–W.
sites.todaySetup: The 300 ft launch faces a challenging morning with northerly gradient contamination before the sea breeze establishes. At 8 AM, surface winds are from 336° (NNW) at 5.3 kts with an 80m layer showing 12.2 kts — a significant wind shear signal indicating the gradient wind aloft hasn't decoupled from the surface yet. By 9–11 AM winds shift toward W/WNW and weaken considerably (1.6–3.5 kts surface), which sounds calm but the BL is only 155–340m deep, meaning little thermal energy. The real sea breeze establishes around noon from 283° at 5 kts and builds to 8.3 kts from 303° by 3 PM. BL height reaches 395–470m in the afternoon, enough for weak thermals off the lower slopes. Zero CAPE and 0% precip confirm no convective energy — this is purely a mechanical/sea breeze day. Afternoon conditions at 300 ft are the most viable but remain light-to-moderate.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNW gradient contamination with strong shear aloft (12.2 kts at 80m vs 5.3 kts surface at 8 AM). Wind direction slowly shifts W by 11 AM but too light and confused for reliable launches. Not recommended.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Sea breeze fills in from WSW–WNW at 5–7.6 kts by noon. BL height 390–420m. Flyable conditions developing by noon–1 PM with weak valley thermals possible. Best window opens here.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 7.6–9.2 kts from 293–318° (WNW–NW), building steadily. BL peaks at 470m around 4 PM then drops. Getting punchier by 3–4 PM; NW component increases rotor risk behind the ridge.

verdict.hazards: Morning NNW gradient contamination with strong wind shear; afternoon wind NW-ing up past 8 kts increases rotor potential; BL cap limits thermal height
verdict.skillLevel: P2+ (noon window only, site check required)

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — mid launch on Mission Peak ridge, W-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is more exposed than the 300 ft and works best with W to WNW sea breeze at 8–15 kts. It handles thermals and ridge lift combined, and is the go-to launch when the valley breeze is well established. Too strong or too northerly and rotor risk behind the ridge becomes serious.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch is clearly impacted by the overnight gradient wind event — at 8 AM it's showing 14.4 kts from 21° (NNE) with 80m winds at 22.8 kts. This is a dangerous, cross/reverse wind condition for a W-facing launch with strong shear. The gradient influence slowly decays through the morning: by 10 AM it's down to 8.1 kts NNE, and by 11 AM it finally rotates to 350° (N) at 4.3 kts and by noon shifts to 325° at 3.8 kts. The sea breeze establishes by 1–2 PM from 299–308° at 3–7 kts, building to 7.6 kts by 3 PM. This is the viable afternoon window, but the NW component (304–318° by 4–5 PM) is a concern for rotor on a W-facing hill. BL height 420–465m in the afternoon supports weak thermals.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Dangerous NNE gradient wind 8–14 kts at surface, 18–23 kts at 80m. Completely wrong direction for this W-facing launch and well above safe limits. Do not launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind shifts from N to NW to WNW through this window. By noon it's 3.8 kts from 325° — still north of ideal. By 1 PM it reaches 305° at 3.1 kts. Transition period; wait for wind to fully establish from W before launching.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 2–4 PM with WNW flow at 7–7.6 kts from 299–308°. Approaching ideal direction and speed for this launch. By 5 PM wind backs to 318° NW at 8.7 kts — getting marginal on direction.

verdict.hazards: Dangerous NNE gradient wind in morning (22+ kts at 80m); slow wind direction transition; NW backing in late afternoon increases rotor risk; shallow BL caps thermals

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — high launch on Mission Peak, NW-facing summit
sites.howItWorks: The 1750 ft high launch requires moderate W to NW winds for ridge lift and is used when thermals are strong enough to work above the ridge. It's a more advanced site requiring confident launch technique and the ability to handle stronger, more variable upper-level winds. Ideal: 10–18 kts W–NW.
sites.todaySetup: The high launch bears the full brunt of the overnight gradient wind — at 8 AM it's reporting 9.3 kts from 29° (NNE) with a dangerous 19.7 kts at 80m, and by 10 AM it's still 11.1 kts NNE with 14.4 kts at 80m. This is wrong direction and excessive shear for any launch attempt. The gradient slowly decays and rotates: by noon it's 6.2 kts from 355° (N), and by 1 PM 4.4 kts from 345° (NNW). The sea breeze finally reaches the high launch by 2 PM from 312° at 5.8 kts, backing slightly to 296° by 4 PM. BL height only reaches 425–430m — well below the 1750 ft (533m) launch elevation — meaning thermals cannot even reach launch height today. This is a critical problem: with the thermal ceiling below launch altitude, there is no thermal energy available and ridge lift will be the only mechanism, at speeds that are borderline for the site.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE gradient wind 7–11 kts surface, 8–20 kts at 80m. Wrong direction, dangerous shear, 95–100% cloud cover. Absolutely no-fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind slowly rotates from N through NW to WNW. At noon still from 355° (N) at 6.2 kts — wrong direction for this launch. Not viable until 1:30–2 PM at earliest.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: WNW flow at 5.8–6.9 kts from 296–312°, which is the correct direction but BL height (395–430m) is below the 533m launch elevation — no thermal support whatsoever. Light ridge lift only, with wind weakening by 5 PM.

verdict.hazards: Dangerous NNE gradient wind all morning (20 kts at 80m); BL height stays below launch elevation all day (430m max vs 533m launch); thermal ceiling too low; slow wind direction transition; NNE shear risk
verdict.skillLevel: P3+ locals only — not recommended today

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday (3/23) looks cleaner at Mussel Rock. Winds are W/WNW at 5–7 kts throughout the day with 0–13% cloud cover — a much better marine layer situation. BL heights reach 320–345m by afternoon. Should be a solid ridge-soaring day with consistent sea breeze. Best window noon–4 PM.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday shows a much calmer morning (5 kts NNW at 8 AM, near-calm by 10 AM) and a clean sea breeze developing from WNW by noon at 3.7 kts building to 5.2–7.4 kts WNW–NW by afternoon. BL height reaches 595–680m — significantly better thermal ceiling than today. Good P2 afternoon conditions, best 1–4 PM.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday morning still shows some residual NNW gradient (7.2 kts at 8 AM from 346°) but weakens to near-calm by 10 AM. Afternoon sea breeze from 298–315° at 3.7–7.4 kts with BL heights 410–680m looks excellent. Better day than Saturday by a wide margin. Best window noon–4 PM.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday morning gradient nearly gone (6.2 kts from 319° at 8 AM, then near-calm at 10 AM). Afternoon WNW flow 3.7–7.4 kts with BL heights reaching 595–680m (above the 533m launch elevation by early afternoon). Thermals can finally reach launch height. Best day of the weekend for the high launch — P3+ pilots should target 1–4 PM.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: No data beyond 3/23 in this dataset. Trend from the pressure gradient pattern suggests the gradient wind event fully clears by Sunday, potentially setting up a clean Monday sea breeze cycle. Monitor.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Day 2 (Monday) data not available. If the gradient wind continues to relax and a thermal high builds, expect improving thermal conditions with longer BL development windows through the week.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Day 2 outlook pending. Sunday's improving trend suggests Monday could offer the best thermal day of the week if the high pressure consolidates and the sea breeze cycle normalizes.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Day 2 outlook pending. If BL heights continue growing as seen in Sunday's data (680m+), Monday could offer the high launch's best window of the period with thermals well above launch altitude.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not available. The late-week pattern should be watched for the next gradient wind cycle or marine layer return. Check updated forecasts Tuesday for Thursday–Friday outlook.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 outlook not available from current data. Spring pattern typically sees afternoon sea breeze solidify by mid-week. Check updated Open-Meteo Tuesday morning.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not available. Likely to follow the improving inland thermal trend if synoptic pattern remains stable. No new gradient events apparent in current data.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not available. Pending a stable synoptic pattern, the high launch should see continued improvement through mid-week as the gradient event fully dissipates.

watchlist: Overnight KHAF gradient wind (30 kt gusts) may linger into Saturday morning — do not trust model calm numbers before site-checking in person
BL height at Ed Levin stays below the 1750 ft launch elevation all day Saturday — thermal ceiling too low for the high launch
Erratic cloud cover at Mussel Rock (swings 1–100% multiple hours) suggests messy airmass; watch for sudden overcast killing ridge lift
Wind direction at Ed Levin 600 ft and 1750 ft doesn't rotate to flyable WNW until early-to-mid afternoon — patience required before launching
Sunday looks significantly better at all Ed Levin launches — consider saving energy for Sunday if Saturday conditions look sketchy on site
tldr: Saturday is a frustrating day — a strong overnight gradient event (30 kt gusts at KHAF) leaves the morning a mess of wrong-direction winds and strong shear at Ed Levin, and the thermal ceiling stays too low for the high launch all day. Mussel Rock and Ed Levin 300/600 ft have a marginal afternoon window (1–3 PM) once the sea breeze establishes, but conditions are light and inconsistent. Skip Saturday or fly Mussel Rock cautiously in the early afternoon — Sunday is the better day with cleaner winds and a higher BL.
